ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Methi Malai Paneer; closest parent is paneer dish (FOODON_03311069).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Methi Malai Paneer; A0CKP (cottage cheese dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Methi Malai Paneer or a sufficiently close parent dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Methi Malai Paneer is approximately 250 g, comprising paneer pieces in a rich fenugreek-cream gravy, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer and Paneer-based preparations; ICMR-NIN composite estimate for cream-based paneer curry with fenugreek
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Methi Malai Paneer as a composite dish. Values are derived by adapting ICMR-NIN data for paneer (raw, per 100 g), cream, and fenugreek leaves, weighted by a standard recipe proportion (paneer ~45%, cream-gravy ~50%, fenugreek ~5%). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for paneer (FDC ID 173944) and heavy cream. This is an estimated composite; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — paneer / Indian cottage cheese and cream-based curry analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Methi Malai Paneer. GI estimated at 27 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for paneer (GI ~27) and cream-based Indian curry analogues. The dish is predominantly fat and protein with modest carbohydrate from onion, tomato, and spices; cream and paneer dominate, suppressing glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (27 × 6.2 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 4.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~775 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 775 mg sodium, representing roughly one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~27) driven by high fat and protein content from paneer and cream
type2_diabetes.guidance: Methi Malai Paneer has a low glycemic index of approximately 27 and a glycemic load of about 4 per serving, making it a suitable occasional choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high saturated fat content warrants attention to overall dietary fat targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~7.2 g per 100 g; ~18 g per restaurant serving) from paneer and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat load from cream and paneer in a full restaurant serving (~18 g saturated fat) may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption in line with clinical dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Methi Malai Paneer is a popular North Indian vegetarian curry in which cubes of fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese) are simmered in a rich, mildly spiced sauce made from cream (malai), fresh or dried fenugreek leaves (methi), onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices including cumin, garam masala, and turmeric. The dish is characterised by its creamy, velvety texture, a subtle bitterness from the fenugreek balanced by the sweetness of cream, and a mild-to-medium heat level. It is a staple of North Indian restaurant menus and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, Methi Malai Paneer is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) with a high fat content (~12.5 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated) and a meaningful protein contribution (~7.8 g per 100 g) from paneer. Its glycemic index is low (~27), making it relatively suitable for glycemic management, though the saturated fat and sodium content require attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. Fenugreek leaves contribute iron, folate, and vitamin A. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible, making it broadly accessible across dietary communities in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Methi Malai Paneer as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer and cream data with recipe-proportion weighting, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using paneer as the closest match. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact dish-level data from an approved primary source is unavailable.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites derived from ICMR-NIN paneer and cream data weighted by standard recipe proportions; they should be treated as indicative rather than exact. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer or cream-based curry preparations. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer-based dishes. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~27) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for paneer; no direct GI measurement for Methi Malai Paneer is available in approved sources. 6. The dish is served at two field survey data restaurants (Mr Tandoor – Boat Quay; Maharani Table – Boat Quay) at SGD 17.90–21.90 per portion. 7. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central paneer and cream data and should be treated as approximate.
